item.title: How to get the most out of AI talks with China
item.description: Dialogue with rivals can be helpful, but plenty of traps lay ahead.

item.title: Is France really poorer than Mississippi?
item.description: Europe’s way of life would decline if America slowed down.

item.title: AI won’t replace lawyers. It will create more of them.
item.description: The legal profession is changing, but the lawyer is here to stay.

item.title: Bill Cassidy and America’s increasingly broken primary system
item.description: The country's democracy suffers as both parties close off elections.

item.title: New grads compete with AI for jobs
item.description: Edith Pritchett cartoon on college graduation, job market.

item.title: Ken Paxton vs. Netflix
item.description: The Texas attorney general pivots hard to the left with an attack on the streamer.

item.title: Teachers are in denial about what their profession has become
item.description: Student attention spans, redistricting and Ted Turner, in readers’ eyes.

item.title: Graham Platner is forcing Dems to change
item.description: Are voters rejecting career politicians? The 2026 midterms are already being shaken up by populist firebrands and antiestablishment outsiders. In this episode of "Make It Make Sense," Kate Andrews, Carine Hajjar and James Desio break down Graham Platner’s controversial momentum in Maine, James Talarico's polished strategy in Texas and Spencer Pratt’s AI-fueled mayoral run in Los Angeles. Are voters ready to overhaul the political elite, or are these candidates just the latest shiny objects in the national culture war?
